02 · Overview

SEO is running. The right people just are not seeing it yet.

Overall, SEO is running and bringing in traffic and clicks, but mostly from people who already know the practice by name. The keywords that actually bring new patients (dentist kitchener, kitchener dentist, dentist in kitchener, best dentist in kitchener) are stuck around position 8-20, not in the Top 5 where clicks happen, where business comes.

This audit will uncover exactly what to fix to move those keywords into the Top 3-5, so the traffic turns into new-patient calls.

Search Console, top queries: brand searches take the clicks, commercial searches sit on impressionsCLICK TO ZOOM +

02 · What is already working

Before the fixes: the site is not starting from zero.
Three real strengths this plan is built on top of.

Strength 1 · Reviews
4.9★
from 880 reviews. That is more reviews than the competitors, and it is real proof patients trust Frederick Dental. This is exactly the kind of trust signal Google and AI both weigh.
Strength 2 · Blog posts earning real clicks

A few blog posts are doing especially well in clicks (GSC, last 3 months):

  • Make Composite Fillings Last Longer · 38 clicks
  • Signs of a Serious Dental Problem · 21 clicks
  • Dental Bridges and Face Shape · 20 clicks
Strength 3 · The brand is growing

More and more people are searching "Frederick Dental" by name, and those brand searches rank #1 and get the clicks. Rising brand demand is one of the strongest long-term signals Google reads, and it tells us the practice is building a name patients remember.

In short: the foundation is strong. The site does not need saving. It needs a few specific blockers removed, and once they are gone, the four keywords that bring new patients have a clear road to the Top 3.

Technical · Schema markup · 1 of 3

Problem 1: Homepage schema has errors.

Problem

Homepage schema has services listed, but they are not defined by schema.org meaning. To schema.org, these labels are basically "what?" It does not recognize them, so it cannot count them. This shows up as an error.

Evidence
Schema validator, frederickdental.ca homepage, service labels erroringCLICK TO ZOOM +
Why it matters

We want to hand Google our menu of services directly, in a language it actually understands. Right now, that menu is written in a label Google does not recognize, so this part of the message never reaches Google at all. It is like handing someone a menu written in a language they cannot read: they can see it, they just cannot understand a word of it.

CURRENT · BROKEN LABEL

"itemOffered": { "@type": "DentalProcedure", "name": "Root Canal Treatment" }

DentalProcedure is not a real word in Google's dictionary. So even though "Root Canal Treatment" is sitting right there, Google cannot read past the broken label to get to it. This is happening for all 9 of our services, not just this one.

Solution

We fix this by swapping in the correct label, the one Google's dictionary actually recognizes, for every service. Same services, same names, just written in a language Google understands this time:

CORRECTED · GOOGLE CAN READ IT

"itemOffered": { "@type": "MedicalProcedure", "name": "Root Canal Treatment" }

Schema knows → Google gets to know.

The full corrected block, ready to review: Fixed Schema Code

Technical · Schema markup · 2 of 3

Problem 2: No doctor entity in the schema.

Problem

Dr. Al Bogha, the dentist who actually runs the practice, does not exist anywhere in that data. No name, no title, no connection to the business record. As far as Google's structured data is concerned, this is a dental practice with no dentist.

Note: what you are seeing in the screenshot is the Dentist schema, which is the local business / organization record, not the doctor's own Person profile. The business exists in the data; Dr. Al Bogha does not.

Evidence
Current homepage schema, plus how it should be (example)CLICK TO ZOOM +
Why it matters

These two things, the doctor and the dentist details, are exactly what make a business feel real, verifiable, and relevant to Google. Right now the site has strong material (a real doctor, a real address) but is not handing any of it to Google in a format it can use.

Solution

Add proper entries for both, one for the doctor, one for the dentist details, so schema knows, and Google gets to know.

How it should be, ready to review: Corrected Schema Code

Opportunity · Be the first dentist in Kitchener with real doctor schema

None of the competitors checked in this audit, including the exact-match domain competitor and every peer site, have implemented Person or Physician schema for their doctors. This is not holding Frederick Dental back today; it is simply unclaimed ground.

Is it necessary? No   Is it worth doing anyway? Yes

If nobody in this local market has done it, it is a real, low-cost differentiator. Combined with the fact that Dr. Al Bogha is a genuine, licensed, verifiable dentist, and the fee is basically free (a JSON-LD block, no design change, no new content required), this is close to the best kind of opportunity there is: nobody is doing it, it costs almost nothing, and it directly reinforces something Google already weighs more heavily for health sites (E-E-A-T). We can leverage it further: a complete doctor entity gives Google the material to build a display card for Dr. Al Bogha, the way Wikipedia-style knowledge panels appear for recognized people.

Technical · Schema markup · 3 of 3

Problem 3: Author is not present in blogs,
and in schema it is not real (authorized).

Evidence

How it should be: Example  ·  Info needed from client: Example

  • Google is moving from understanding websites → understanding entities (people, organizations, places, products).
  • A detailed Person schema helps establish the dentist as a trusted entity.
  • This supports Google's understanding of expertise (E-E-A-T), improves knowledge connections across the web, and can strengthen visibility in both traditional search and AI-powered search experiences.
  • That will make our citation probability stronger.
Technical · Schema markup · The benchmark

A competitor is already speaking fluent machine.
Frederick can speak it better.

Right now Frederick Dental's markup is thin. A direct local competitor, kitchenerkrowndental.com, already runs a rich schema stack (validated on the right): Dentist / LocalBusiness / MedicalOrganization, MedicalProcedure (11 items), FAQPage, HowTo, Organization, Place, WebPage. 18 items, zero errors.

That is exactly the kind of structured data that both classic search and AI answer engines (AI Overviews, ChatGPT, Gemini) read to decide who to trust and cite.

Why it matters: better entity signals strengthen trust (E-E-A-T), improve eligibility for rich search results, and make it easier for AI search experiences to identify and reference Frederick Dental as an authoritative source.

What we can do: we can build a complete digital profile for Dr. Al Bogha and his place of practice. Expand the profile with qualifications, professional memberships, years of experience, and structured data for the dentist, practice, services, and process. This gives search engines and AI platforms clear, consistent information about who the practice is and what it specializes in.

kitchenerkrowndental.com, validated schema stack, 18 items, 0 errorsCLICK TO ZOOM +

Technical · Core Web Vitals · The honest read

Speed will not rank the site by itself.
But it quietly blocks ranking, and it loses patients at the door.

Why it matters
  • Speed is a minor ranking signal and mainly acts as a tie-breaker: something that does not help ranking, but can block ranking.
  • Competitor data confirms this: one competitor still outranks Frederick Dental despite having poor performance metrics.
  • However, a faster website improves user experience, reduces abandonment, and helps convert more visitors into patients.
Solution
  • We need to compress and properly size images.
  • Need to reduce render-blocking CSS and JavaScript.
  • We need to improve caching and hosting performance.
  • All of these are achievable.
Technical · The quiet leaks

Other technical issues
The kind that slowly kill the overall SEO effort.

Problem

Crawled but not indexed: Google has discovered and crawled these pages but not indexed them. This leads to either the content being read as generic or of low quality.

Evidence
URLStatus
/blog/are-composite-fillings-good-for-cavities-between-teeth-what-you-should-know/Crawled, not indexed
/blog/dental-emergency-or-can-it-wait/Crawled, not indexed
/blog/dental-cleanings-heart-health-diabetes-wellness/Crawled, not indexed
/blog/composite-vs-porcelain-restorations-which-option-gives-you-the-best-long-term-smile/Crawled, not indexed
/blog/dental-implants-vs-tooth-replacement-options/Crawled, not indexed
Solution

These URLs need internal linking: contextual links from related service pages and blog posts, so Google sees the site itself vouching for them.

Technical · Content publishing

Batch Content Publishing Causing Weak Crawl & Indexation Follow-Through

Issue

The website follows a repeated batch publishing pattern, where multiple blogs are published on the same day each month. For example, blogs were published together in April, May, and again on June 17, 2026. However, the June blogs are still not indexed, which suggests that new content is being published without a strong crawl and indexation process.

Evidence
Why Baby Teeth Matter · published June 17, 2026 · same-day batchCLICK TO ZOOM +
All-On-4 Dental Implants · published June 17, 2026 · same-day batchCLICK TO ZOOM +
Seasonal Sports and Orthodontic Care · published June 17, 2026 · same-day batchCLICK TO ZOOM +
Why it matters

If new blogs are not indexed, they cannot rank, drive traffic, or support the main service pages through topical authority. This also shows that content may be treated as a monthly deliverable, rather than being properly published, linked, submitted, and tracked as an SEO asset.

Solution

Move to a structured content calendar instead of same-day batch publishing. Each blog should be published with internal links, sitemap inclusion, GSC submission, and indexation checks after 3, 7, and 14 days. If a page remains unindexed, review crawlability, content quality, canonical tags, internal links, and page value.

Technical · Expected outcomes

The expected outcomes on our website

Technical SEO fixes
(Core Web Vitals + Schema + Sitemap + Indexing)
↓
Google and AI understand the website better
↓
Better crawl and indexing efficiency
↓
Stronger technical authority
↓
Higher ranking potential

Note: finally, if two websites have similar authority, backlink profiles, and content quality, the one with stronger technical SEO is more likely to perform better in search results. Technical optimization gives search engines greater confidence in the website, increasing its chances of achieving higher rankings and stronger visibility in both traditional search and AI-powered search experiences.

Off page · Problem 1

We have over-optimized our backlink anchor text
with exact-match keywords.

Evidence
MetricResultSource
Total backlinks (site-wide)1.7KSemrush
Referring domains79Semrush
Backlinks checked in detail69Manually verified
Dofollow links (in sample)61 (90%)Manually verified
Keyword-stuffed ("dentist Kitchener," "best dentist in Kitchener," etc.)45 (66%) riskManually verified
Natural (brand name, plain URL, "visit website")23 (34%) healthyManually verified
Semrush domain overview, Jul 5, 2026: 1.7K backlinks, 79 referring domainsCLICK TO ZOOM +

The screenshot backs the table: 1.7K backlinks across 79 referring domains, straight from the Semrush domain overview.

And every link we checked by hand, anchor by anchor: Backlinks sheet (Semrush export)

Why it matters

Too much over-optimization is risky, because if Google's systems detect this exact-match anchor pattern happening repeatedly, they can flag it as unnatural and stop counting it as a genuine trust signal. Once that happens, it does not just affect the flagged links, it can make Google cautious about the site's whole backlink profile going forward. So even if we keep building new backlinks after this, Google will not necessarily give them full credit, the trust just will not pass through the way it should.

Solution
  • We need to be smarter about when to use exact keywords in an anchor and when not to. On link insertions we can target long-tail, semantic phrases (things like "learn more about emergency dental care") instead of the exact head keyword every time.
  • On bigger, high-authority sites, we should aim for brand mentions ("Frederick Dental") rather than keyword anchors.
  • On guest posts, exact-match anchors can still be used, but sparingly, since guest posts are naturally where a bit of intentional anchor text is expected. If we balance it this way across link types, Google will not hold back the value of our off-page efforts and we get the full benefit of the work we put in.

Off page · Problem 2 · The fix

Why the links died,
and how the next ones will not.

Why it matters

When the page carrying our backlink was indexed, we were getting its ranking benefit passed to our site. Now that Google has dropped that page from its index, Google is not crawling or counting it anymore, so we have lost that benefit even though the link itself is technically still there.

Solution
  • Choose better sites going forward. We should choose the link-insertion method and only target sites and articles that have already been indexed and stable for at least two years. A page that has survived that long, through multiple Google updates, has already proven Google trusts it, so it is far less likely to lose its index status after we get a link from it.
  • Choose relevant sites, not just any site. As we discussed in the previous two audits, the sweet spot is domains that are about health and dental, or a little broader, as long as the domain itself does not point at some other niche. Take one of our current live links, hop-traveler.com: the site already carries plenty of health and dental articles, but the name itself tells Google this domain is about travelers. When the domain's identity points at one niche, content from another niche on it runs a high chance of getting de-indexed down the line, so ideally we still avoid it. Neutral domains that signal no niche at all (a general magazine, for example) are also fine to use, provided the other metrics hold up: spam score, real traffic, and index stability.
The vetting checklist, every future placement
  • Indexed and stable for 2+ years, survived multiple Google updates.
  • Domain identity fits: health or dental, a little broader, or a neutral name that signals no niche. Never a name that points at a different niche.
  • Clean metrics: low spam score, real organic traffic, steady index status.

The niche-relevant links competitors have

dentistlist.orgKitchener listingdentalsolutionskitchener.ca

Canadian dental directory		Dentist profilewalkintheparkfamilydental.com

Canadian dental directory		Practice profiledentalsolutionskitchener.ca
nomorewaitlists.netDoctor directory listingwalkintheparkfamilydental.com
datagemba.comKitchener cosmetic dentists listwalkintheparkfamilydental.com
Why it matters

In the dental (YMYL / health) space, where a link comes from matters more than how many links you have. A listing on a dental-specific directory or a mention in dental trade media tells Google "other people in this exact industry recognize this practice," which is a stronger trust signal than another generic local-business directory. Frederick currently lacks presence in this specific category.

Solution

We need to submit Frederick Dental to the dental-specific directories confirmed above (dentistlist.org, dentalnearyou.ca or a Canadian dental directory equivalent, canadian.dental if it accepts new listings), and we should outreach to dental trade or health-focused publications: guest posts that only talk about health, rather than more generic local-business directories.

On page and content · Problem 1

Service pages repeat the same boilerplate
across every page.

Problem

For example: /service/emergency-dentist-kitchener-on/ and /service/dental-implants-kitchener-on/ share:

  • The same "We strive to..." mission block
  • The same insurance / CDCP paragraph
  • The same "Why Choose Our Practice" section
  • The same three patient testimonials
  • The same footer

Similarly, all service pages follow the same kind of theme and content, and read generic, AI-ish too.

Evidence
Service page, repeated blocks, example ACLICK TO ZOOM +
Service page, same blocks, example BCLICK TO ZOOM +
Service page, same mission and testimonials, example CCLICK TO ZOOM +
Service page, same insurance block, example DCLICK TO ZOOM +
Why it matters

When two pages read almost the same, both people and Google notice. First, a patient scanning the site sees the same lines repeated and it feels less trustworthy, like nobody wrote this for them specifically. Second, Google sees near-duplicate content across pages, which makes it harder for any one page to stand out and rank. And AI tools looking for a clear, specific answer skip over pages that just repeat generic marketing lines.

Solution

We can keep the boilerplate blocks (mission, CDCP, testimonials) but shrink them, and use the space we free up to add real, treatment-specific content: what to expect, cost and insurance details for that specific service, and common questions patients actually ask about it. We can give the complete revamped page as an example after this audit. If you say so, we would be happy to do it :)

On page and content · Problem 2

Two of the four target keywords
never appear on the homepage at all.

0
occurrences of "dentist kitchener" on the homepage (1,300 searches / mo)
0
occurrences of "best dentist in kitchener" on the homepage (210 searches / mo)
Problem

That does not mean we should fill the page with keyword stuffing. Instead, by researching the content gap against competitors, we can add those sections. Something that is best for on-page, for the user, for Google, for AI models. Sections dedicated to:

  • Now Accepting New Patients
  • A process / step section
  • Hours, Location, First Visit section

Let me show you some of the competition's content and smart tactics.

Evidence
Competitor 1, kitchenerkrowndental.com, homepage sectionsCLICK TO ZOOM +
Competitor 1, kitchenerkrowndental.com, process and new-patient blocksCLICK TO ZOOM +
Competitor 2, kitchenerfamilydentist.com, homepage sectionCLICK TO ZOOM +

COMPETITOR 1: kitchenerkrowndental.com  ·  COMPETITOR 2: kitchenerfamilydentist.com

Why it matters

When someone searches using those exact words, the homepage has nothing written in that specific phrasing to match against. It is not a content gap, it is a wording gap, and it is an easy one to close with a new, relevant content opportunity.

Solution

Rather than inserting the missing phrases directly, we can build new sections the way competitors have, placed wherever they best fit the natural flow of the page, and written with the patient's mindset in mind at each point (what are they wondering right here, what would reassure them). Within those sections, we also bring in what makes Frederick Dental genuinely different, not copying a competitor's section, but shaping our own version of it. Done this way, the missing phrasing shows up naturally as part of real, useful content, not as something forced in.

On page and content · Problem 3

Except the FAQ, the homepage lacks touchpoints
where AI can crawl easily.
AI extractability. In 2026, this is non-negotiable, and today it is a missed opportunity.

Problem
Homepage heading map: after H2 comes H4, H1 followed by H3sCLICK TO ZOOM +

Right now the structure is not proper: after an H2 comes an H4, and the H1 is followed by H3s. The content is somewhere good in terms of explaining, but for AI bots it is flat information, no answer-first blocks, no clean hierarchy they can lift from.

Why it matters

AI bots look for answer-first, clean structure: content written in a way where they get exactly what they need to feed their own knowledge. During grounding, one of the processes that decides who gets cited in an AI answer, that structure is what makes them choose our website over the next one.

Evidence

Similarly, all the service pages follow the same kind of boilerplate, the same "We strive to..." and CDCP blocks page after page:

Pediatric service page · same We strive to and CDCP blocksCLICK TO ZOOM +

/service/pediatric-dentistry-kitchener-on/

Dental cleanings service page · same blocks againCLICK TO ZOOM +

/service/dental-cleanings-exams-kitchener-on/

Solution

Rebuild the structure so every section is liftable: proper heading order, answer-first blocks, content written once for the user, once for SEO, and once for the machine, all in the same page. As of now, we have worked on the homepage to give you a glimpse of how the content looks for the user, for SEO, and for AI extraction:

Revamped homepage · live preview →

Opportunities · Content architecture

Pillar cluster strategy.
Six scattered Invisalign posts, zero authoritative answers.

Why it is an opportunity: right now, someone searching "invisalign kitchener" or asking ChatGPT "can Invisalign fix an overbite?" finds six scattered articles, none of which is built to be the answer. Google sees six partial pages instead of one authoritative source, so no single page accumulates enough weight to rank for the broad, commercial term. A pillar changes that: it gives Google one comprehensive page to rank, gives AI systems one page worth citing in full, and passes authority down to the service page where bookings actually happen.

How we do it:

  • Create the pillar page ("Invisalign in Kitchener: The Complete Guide"), one comprehensive page answering the umbrella question, written by the credentialed doctor (ties into the E-E-A-T fix).
  • Link the pillar down to all six existing posts, each with a descriptive anchor.
  • Edit each of the six posts to link back up to the pillar (one contextual link each).
  • Link the pillar to the existing service page (/service/invisalign-kitchener-on/) so commercial intent flows where it converts.

Once this proves out, we can repeat the same model for the next-strongest cluster: Pediatric, 5 posts. We verified each of the five, and none is positioned to become the hub, so the pediatric cluster needs a new pillar page created from scratch, the same as Invisalign, rather than expanding an existing post into one.

HOW IT LOOKS:

Pillar and cluster structure: pillar links down to posts, posts link up, pillar feeds the service pageCLICK TO ZOOM +

THE EVIDENCE, POST BY POST · WE READ ALL FIVE:

PostVerified contentHub?
Choosing the right dentist for your childBuyer's guide: how to choose a dentistNo
When should your child first see a dentist (the 1-1-1 rule)Narrow: first-visit timing ruleNo
Hidden sugars and child teethNarrow: diet / sugar sourcesNo
Why baby teeth matterNarrow: why primary teeth matterNo
How to prevent cavities in kidsNarrow: 9 prevention tipsNo
  • Also found while verifying: authorship gaps, no credentialed author on the posts (the same E-E-A-T fix from the schema section).
  • Content overlap between posts, splitting the same queries across pages.
  • Inconsistent cross-linking: the five posts barely reference each other.
Bringing it together · 1 of 2

So how does all of this rank our four target keywords?
Every fix in this audit points at the same four words.

The four targets (dentist kitchener, kitchener dentist, dentist in kitchener, best dentist in kitchener) are stuck at position 8-20 today. Here is the plain-English chain from each fix to a higher ranking.

What we fixWhat it doesHow that lifts the target keywords
Schema + doctor + author
Technical		Tells Google clearly who the practice is, who the dentist is, and that a real credentialed person stands behind the content.Google trusts the whole site more (E-E-A-T). A more trusted site is allowed to rank higher for competitive terms like "dentist kitchener", not just brand searches.
Core Web Vitals + indexing
Technical		Removes the speed tie-breaker that can hold a page back, and gets stranded blog pages counted again through internal links.Clears the blockers so the homepage and service pages are free to climb, and passes internal trust to the pages targeting these keywords.
Clean, relevant backlinks
Off page		Fixes over-optimized anchors, replaces lost links with aged, dental-relevant ones, and adds the niche directories competitors already have.Off-page trust is what separates position 8 from position 3 on head terms. This is the lever that moves the needle most on the four commercial keywords.
Unique, specific page content
On page		Ends the boilerplate so each service page reads as its own page, and adds the "dentist kitchener" wording naturally through real new sections.Closes the wording gap on the homepage and lets each page stand out, so Google has a clear, specific page to rank for each exact search.
Existing strengths
Already live		4.9★ from 880 reviews, blog posts already earning real clicks, brand searches growing and ranking #1.These give the fixes a head start. We are lifting a trusted, review-strong practice the last few positions, not building reputation from scratch.

In one line: technical earns trust, off page proves trust, on page gives Google a clear page to reward, and the reviews and brand momentum make it move faster. Stacked together, that is what carries the four keywords from 8-20 into the Top 3-5.

Bringing it together · 2 of 2

And how does all of this get us cited more in AI?
AI answers cite trusted, clearly-labelled entities. We are building exactly that.

AI Overviews, ChatGPT, and Gemini do not read a website the way a person does. They look for a clear, verifiable entity (a real practice, a real dentist) and quote sources they can trust. Every part of this audit makes Frederick Dental easier for them to identify and safer for them to cite.

Give AI a clear entity to name
  • Doctor + Person + practice schema makes Dr. Al Bogha and the practice a defined entity AI can recognize and attribute, instead of an anonymous page.
  • Real, authored content gives AI a credentialed source to point to, which raises citation probability in a YMYL health topic where trust matters most.
Give AI content worth quoting
  • Rich schema stack (matching or beating the competitor's 18-item markup) is the structured data AI reads to decide who to trust.
  • Pillar pages + unique service content give AI one comprehensive, specific source to cite in full, instead of six scattered half-answers.
Give AI reasons to trust the source
  • Backlinks from trusted, relevant sites also help here: AI systems lean on the same authority signals Google does, so links from dental directories and health publications raise the odds of being cited.
  • A clean link profile (the off-page fixes) means nothing in the profile argues against trusting the practice.
Clear entity + rich schema + real authored content + links from trusted sites
↓
AI can identify Frederick Dental and trust the source
↓
Higher chance of being named and cited in AI answers for Kitchener dental questions

The same work does double duty: the trust and clarity that lift the four keywords in classic search are the exact signals that get the practice named in AI answers. One plan, two front doors, both pointing new patients to Frederick Dental.

The engine

Four fixes. One engine. Each one feeds the next, and rankings are the output.

Where this goes next

Thank you.
